Han asked John about his weekend plans. John explained he would be spending time with his Christian brothers. 
Han expressed surprise, noting John's appearance didn't align with his perceived image of a religious person. John countered that many Americans, including scientists, are religious.  
Han questioned the compatibility of science and religion, but John affirmed their coexistence, highlighting the U.S.'s status as both a scientifically advanced and religiously devout nation.  
Han struggled to understand how people could believe in God when evidence suggested otherwise. John suggested that understanding this would come with a deeper knowledge of U.S. history, as religion has been an integral part of American life.
He pointed out that even Harvard University, known for its academic rigor, has roots in religion, having been founded by Puritan immigrants seeking religious freedom. Han inquired about the absence of atheist educators during Harvard's founding, to which John explained that the majority of early English immigrants to the U.S. were Puritans due to religious persecution in 16th and 17th century England.  
John elaborated on the Puritans' desire to establish a haven for their faith in the New World, leading to the founding of Harvard College. The Puritans, having received a classical education in England, aimed to provide the same opportunities for their descendants.
John revealed that Harvard was initially named "New College" before being renamed after benefactor John Harvard, a clergyman. He concluded that religion's influence on Harvard's history was undeniable, suggesting it wouldn't exist without religion.
Han acknowledged his limited knowledge of Harvard's religious underpinnings and the need to learn more about U.S. history and culture to gain a deeper understanding.  John agreed, emphasizing the close relationship between the two.  



Han asked John, "What are your plans for the weekend?"

"I'm going to visit Harvard University," John replied. "I'm planning to apply there after high school, so I want to learn more about it. What about you?"

"I meet with my Christian brothers on Saturdays," John said.

"Oh my God, John! I didn't know you were religious. You seem like a scientific geek, like Leonard from The Big Bang Theory."

"But I'm surprised. I thought science and religion were incompatible."

"Why were they religious?" Han pressed.

"In the 16th and 17th centuries, there were religious reforms in England," John said. "Many religious separatists were persecuted and fled to other countries."

"So there wasn't religious freedom in England?" Han asked.

"Why did they want to start a college?" Han asked.

"Many Puritans had received a classical education at Oxford and Cambridge universities in England," John said. "They wanted their descendants to have the same opportunities."

"I understand," Han said.

"Initially, Harvard was called 'New College' without a formal name," John added.

"When did it become Harvard University?" Han asked.

"In 1639, it was renamed after its major benefactor, John Harvard. Interestingly, John Harvard was a clergyman," John revealed.

"It seems Harvard was destined to be influenced by religion," Han mused.

"It's not an exaggeration to say that without religion, there wouldn't be Harvard," John stated.
